data.title: Kremlin says Putin could meet Zelenskiy if certain agreements are reached
data.description:
The Kremlin said on Saturday that Russian President Vladimir Putin could meet Ukraine's Volodymyr Zelenskiy, but only if certain agreements were reached.

Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov did not specify what agreements would be required from Russia's point of view. Putin and Zelenskiy have not met since December 2019.

President Zelenskiy had challenged the Kremlin leader to meet him in Turkey this week but Putin instead sent a team of aides and officials to meet Ukrainian negotiators on Friday for the first bilateral, face-to-face talks since March 2022.

Ukraine said it had raised the issue of a Putin-Zelenskiy meeting at the talks.

Peskov said Russia considered such a meeting was possible, but only as a result of work between the two sides to "achieve certain results in the form of agreements".

He added: "At the same time, when signing documents that the delegations are to agree upon, the main and fundamental thing for us remains who exactly will sign these documents from the Ukrainian side."

Peskov did not elaborate on that remark. Putin has previously challenged Zelenskiy's legitimacy as president because his elected term of office expired last year.

Ukraine, under martial law as it defends itself against Russia, has not set a date for a new election.

data.title: These Altcoins Plunge Hard but Bitcoin (BTC) Maintains $103K (Market Watch)
data.description:
Bitcoin’s price slipped below $103,000 earlier today, but the bulls managed to defend that level, and the asset is back well above it now.

However, several altcoins have marked massive losses over the past day, led by another double-digit price plunge from PI.BTC Stays Calm

Bitcoin started the business week on the right foot as its price shot up from under $104,000 to a multi-month peak of just shy of $106,000. This came as a direct consequence of the trade deal struck by the US and China.

However, the asset couldn’t maintain its run and dropped by roughly five grand in the following hours to a weekly low of under $101,000. Nevertheless, thebullsdidn’t allow a breakdown beneath $100,000, and the cryptocurrency began its recovery that pushed it to $105,000 by Thursday.

Another rejectionfollowed, and more volatility ensued on Friday, but overall, bitcoin has been able to remain in a relatively tight range between $102,500 and $104,000. The past 24 hours brought some more minor fluctuations around these levels, and BTC now stands close to the upper boundary.

Its market cap has remained above $2.050 trillion on CG while its dominance over the alts has risen by over 0.5% daily to 60.4%.PI Keeps Dumping

Most larger-cap alts have turned red in the past 24 hours. ETH has slipped below $2,500 after a 3% daily decline. A similar nosedive is evident from DOGE, while SHIB and LINK have dropped by over 4%.

However, PI leads the pack in terms of the biggest daily losses. Pi Network’s native token has plummeted by 20% and sits below $0.7.

Other larger-cap alts in the red today include PEPE, UNI, ONDO, AAVE, NEAR, APT, and more.

The total crypto market cap has seen over $70 billion disappear in a day and is down to $3.4 trillion on CG.

data.title: Bitcoin Outshines All In 2025, Official Report From Russian Central Bank Says
data.description:
Russia’s Central Bank reports that Bitcoin has outpaced more traditional investments so far in 2025. Returns clocked in at nearly 40% over the past year. That makes it the top performer compared with gold, stocks and bonds. It’s a sign that more people in Moscow are warming up to cryptocurrencies for their daily money moves.

Bitcoin Leads 2025 Rally

According to the Central Bank of Russia, Bitcoin’s cumulative return since 2022 has reached 121%. That towers over any gains seen in gold or corporate bonds during the same period.

While gold managed only single‑digit increases, Bitcoin has nearly doubled its value in three years. That jump has grabbed the attention of investors who are used to slower returns from banks or stock funds.

Sharp Swings Test Investors

Based on reports from the central bank, Bitcoin’s price swung wildly in early 2025. The first four months brought nearly 20% drop. Many traders faced nerve‑racking weeks as prices slid. But April saw a strong comeback.

Bitcoin climbed more than 10% that month, easing worries among those who watched its dip. Those fast moves highlight just how far digital coins can swing in a short time.

ETFs And Adoption Drive Growth

The rise of spot Bitcoin exchange‑traded funds has made it easier for bigger investors to get in. In places like the United States and Hong Kong, new ETFs let people buy Bitcoin through their regular brokerage accounts.

That convenience has helped push demand higher. More folks don’t have to wrestle with crypto wallets or complex trading platforms anymore. They can tap Bitcoin through tools they already use for stocks and bonds.Local And Global Factors Matter

Global uncertainty has driven some of this interest. Big swings in currency values and low yields on bank deposits have left people hunting for better returns. In Russia, a weaker ruble has nudged locals toward assets like Bitcoin that are priced in dollars.

At the same time, countries such as Kyrgyzstan and Ukraine are exploring crypto in their budgets. Even firms like Cantor Fitzgerald are looking at Bitcoin as a way to shield against market swings.Profit Potential Meets Risk

Based on what the Central Bank found, Bitcoin paid off handsomely for investors who held through the drops. A 38% gain over a year can’t be matched by most safe havens. Yet anyone tracking the 19% fall in early 2025 knows it isn’t for the faint of heart.

Digital coins can zoom higher one month and skid lower the next. That volatility is why many experts still advise limiting how much of your portfolio is in crypto.

Featured image from Unsplash, chart from TradingView

data.title: Japan mulls subsidies for Tesla's EV charging stations in tariff talks, TBS says
data.description:
Japan is considering a plan to offer subsidies for building charging stations for Tesla electric vehicles in tariff talks with the United States, broadcaster TBS reported on Saturday.

The government currently provides subsidies for installing charging stations for EVs only for the charging standard "CHAdeMO", which originated in Japan, but not for Tesla's "Supercharger", the report said.

The U.S. Trade Representative (USTR) has raised concerns about this issue and is requesting improvements, TBS said.

Japan is seeking a third round of U.S. trade talks next week and the nation's top trade negotiator, Ryosei Akazawa, could travel to Washington.

Separately, the Nikkei business daily reported that Japanese negotiators have signalled a willingness to discuss a reduction in U.S. tariffs on Japan, which include 25% on automobile and auto parts and 24% on other goods, rather than pushing Washington to eliminate them completely.

Akazawa has repeatedly said Tokyo wanted full removal of the U.S. tariffs on Japan. The country's priority has been to win exemptions from U.S. tariffs on automobile and auto parts - the key sector in its export-heavy economy.

data.title: China's LandSpace launches improved methane-powered rocket
data.description:
Key points:
  • Cheaper, cleaner methane key to Beijing-based firm's plan to develop reusable rocket
  • LandSpace looking to test its first reusable rocket soon
  • Launch put into orbit satellites developed by US-sanctioned Chinese entity

A new methane-powered rocket developed by China's LandSpace Technology launched six satellites into orbit on Saturday, as the private startup doubles down on a cheap, cleaner fuel that it hopes will help it develop reusable rockets.

The Zhuque-2E Y2 carrier rocket blasted off at 12:12 p.m. (0412 GMT) from the Jiuquan Satellite Launch Center in northwest China, marking the fifth flight for the Zhuque-2 series, according to a company statement.

Beijing-based LandSpace became the world's first company to launch a methane-liquid oxygen rocket in July 2023, ahead of U.S. rivals including Elon Musk's SpaceX and Jeff Bezos' Blue Origin.

Interest has grown in recent years in launching carrier vehicles fuelled by methane, which is deemed less polluting, safer and cheaper than more commonly used hydrocarbon fuels, and a suitable propellant in a reusable rocket.

LandSpace has increased the rocket's payload, reflecting increasing demand in China's expanding commercial space industry amid growing competition to form a constellation of satellites as an alternative to Musk's Starlink.

Its first successful methane-powered launch did not carry any real satellites, but the second launch in December 2023 successfully sent into orbit three satellites. Saturday's launch put six satellites into orbit, mainly developed by Chinese firm Spacety, also known as Changsha Tianyi Space Science and Technology Research Institute.

Li Xiaoming, the institute's vice-president, said in a livestream hosted by LandSpace before the launch that the payload comprised a radar satellite, two multispectral satellites and three satellites for scientific experiments, weighing between 20 kg and 300 kg (44-660 pounds).

REUSABLE ROCKETS

The three research-focussed satellites will help with China's deep-space exploration ambitions, while the pair of multispectral satellites will be dedicated to environmental monitoring and identifying mineral deposits, respectively, Li said.

The radar satellite is an all-weather Earth-observation satellite that produces images during the day and night, as well as see through clouds and rain, he added.

The radar satellite "can also pick up small, millimetre-level shifts in the surface, a capability that makes it extremely useful across urban development, transportation and energy infrastructure monitoring," said Li.

Spacety was sanctioned by the U.S. Treasury Department in January 2023 for allegedly supplying a Russian company with radar satellite imagery over Ukraine, which the U.S. said was used to enable the Russian mercenary group Wagner's combat operations in Ukraine.

Spacety has denied the claims, saying it has never had any business dealings with the entities mentioned by the Treasury Department and that its products and services are solely intended for commercial and civilian use.

Reusable rockets, pioneered by SpaceX, have demonstrated that they can lower costs for launch vehicles and space transportation. LandSpace founder and CEO Zhang Changwu has said the company had started developing reusable rockets and expected to conduct a test launch in the second half of 2025.

The latest model in its Zhuque-2 series includes technical improvements that will help the company's goal of launching a reusable rocket.

Saturday's launch marked the first time LandSpace has deployed a propulsion method that involves chilling both liquid oxygen and methane below their boiling points, boosting thrust.

Chinese commercial space firms have rushed into the sector since 2014, when the government allowed private investment in the industry. LandSpace was one of the earliest and best-funded entrants.

Founded in 2015, LandSpace has secured funding from investors including venture capital firm HongShan, known at that time as Sequoia Capital China, the investment arm of Chinese property developer Country Garden and the state-backed China SME Development Fund.

LandSpace raised 900 million yuan ($120 million) in December from a state-owned fund focussed on advanced manufacturing, while in 2020 it raised 1.2 billion yuan ($170 million), Chinese corporate databases showed.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

2234 GMT - Charter Communications' plans to takeover Cox Communications merges the company with the best in the business, Raymond James analysts said in a research note. The FCC and Justice Department appear open to cable mergers, so the deal should go through without a hitch, but investors looking for Charter to acquire Altice or Cable One will need to dial back such hopes, the analysts said. It's unlikely the company would undergo two major transactions at the same time, and given the Cox assets are an option, it's not surprising the company would choose this strategy over the others, the analysts said. "Cox has always been held up as the gold standard in the industry," they said. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

2139 GMT - Charter Communications' plans to acquire rival Cox Communications will give it greater scale to fend off competition, particularly in the commercial telecommunications market, Morgan Stanley analysts said in a research note. Cox was likely the first U.S. cable operator to push into the market, and not just into small- and medium-sized businesses, but also larger enterprises, the analysts said. As a result, it may have a larger B2B segment relative to its overall business than Charter, which should give the combined company greater scale to compete with current telecom incumbents, the analysts said. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1532 GMT - The big selloff in Doximity shares tied to its conservative outlook feels draconian and misses the bigger picture on the strides Doximity made during fiscal 2025, Raymond James analysts say in a research note. The guidance feels appropriately de-risked given uncertainty in the wider economy, there have been no emerging signs of weakness in its market and backlog growth entering the year appears to be up a healthy 20%, the analysts say. The company seems to have made progress in fiscal 2025 on its client portal, new products and multi-module buying, which have the analysts more confident about the company's growth rate. The analysts maintain an outperform rating. Shares are down 14% at $50.16. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1508 GMT - Doximity's fiscal 2025, which ended March 31, was a strong growth year that fiscal 2026 will have a tough time topping, Mizuho analysts say in a research note. Sales rose 20% from strong revenue retention, particularly among Doximity's top 20 customers. The provider of a networking platform for healthcare professionals now has 116 customers contributing more than $500 million in annual subscription revenue, and those customers account for 84% of FY25's revenue, lowering the risk of small-customer churn, the analysts say. Doximity also grew revenue by 2 to 3 percentage points by shifting to more multi-module integrated offerings, they say. That tough comparison with FY25, along with rising macro uncertainty, explains why Doximity is only guiding for half as much revenue growth in FY26, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1457 GMT - Applied Materials' results and guidance were in-line, but JPMorgan is scaling back estimates. "We are lowering our FY/CY25 revenue/EPS estimates as we account for a weaker 2H demand profile driven by tariff/trade related dynamics for the overall semi/semicap industry," say analysts Harlan Sur and Peter Peng. The semiconductor-equipment company suggested it's not seeing any pull-forward of demand from future quarters due to tariff-led/trade dynamics nor have customers forecasts changed meaningfully, they add. Applied Materials slides 7%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1451 GMT - There will be a debate around how conservative Doximity's guidance is until the company's typical upselling season kicks off in August, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. At that point, Doximity will have better clarity into how the budgets of its pharmaceutical clients are shaping up, the analysts say. As of right now, the company's outlook assumes some weakness in cross-selling and upselling, they say. But management says it hasn't seen any indications yet of its market slowing, and that Doximity faces less risk than it used to now that just under 70% of the year's revenue is already under contract, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1442 GMT - Doximity hasn't seen any signs of its market slowing yet, but is giving conservative guidance on the year ahead in case shifting trade policies weaken the wider economy. "There is a big cloud of policy uncertainty that I think we're all assuming will continue this year," CEO Jeff Tangney says on a call with analysts. The telehealth company faced hard times coming out of the pandemic as doctors went back to the office and the economy stuttered. It learned from that experience that upselling to its pharma clients can become more variable when the macro visibility weakens, CFO Anna Bryson says on the call. Doximity is therefore guiding for its clients' budget growth to be on the lower side of its previous outlook, Bryson says. Doximity slides 15% on the soft outlook. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1420 GMT - Applied Materials has good visibility and confidence on its 2030 roadmap despite near-term growth being mixed, say KeyBanc Capital Markets analysts. The semiconductor-equipment company issued 2Q EPS above estimates and forecast in-line revenue for 3Q. The analysts think Applied Materials took a relatively conservative tone toward near-term conditions but expressed confidence to deep customer relationships and take share on important node transitions toward $1 trillion to $1.3 trillion by 2030. "For now, the market continues to be driven by AI-related logic-and-memory devices, while trailing edge and China remain challenging," they add. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1341 GMT - Applied Materials sinks 6% in early trading. Raymond James says the semiconductor-equipment maker's F2Q revenue and F3Q outlook were largely in-line with consensus while EPS is trending better on margin strength. "Lack of revenue upside is somewhat surprising given the recent results from peers," analyst Srini Pajjuri adds, which he attributes to the company's Internet of Things, Communications, Automotive, Power and Sensors [ICAPS] exposure. But Pajjuri says demand remains healthy in other markets as generative AI continues to drive spending. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1305 GMT - Take-Two Interactive Software delivered a strong finish to fiscal 2025 thanks to broad based performance across core franchises and mobile, says Benchmark's Mike Hickey in a research note. The analyst says interest in the latest installment in the Grand Theft Auto franchise is building, too, "reinforcing the franchise's global cultural resonance and the anticipated impact of its fiscal 2027 release." The video game developer earlier this month delayed the launch of "Grand Theft Auto VI" to May 26 of next year, rather than this coming fall as previously announced. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1247 GMT - Apple, having managed much of its supply chain disruption from the tariff war, can now focus on unveiling an AI strategy roadmap that will lead to "a renaissance of growth," Wedbush analysts say in a research note. The tech giant is expected to announce Alibaba as its AI partner in China prior to Apple's developer's conference in June, the analysts say. "This would lay the groundwork for a global AI strategy to take place over the next year with China a key piece of the growth puzzle," the analysts say. At the conference, investors should hear more about Apple's planned features and rollout of more Apple Intelligence, they say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1233 GMT - Coinbase Global is facing a challenge or two but nothing that should substantially impact its position as one of the primary beneficiaries of institutional adoption of crypto, Benchmark's Mark Palmer says in a research note. The U.S. cryptocurrency exchange Thursday disclosed a significant cybersecurity breach and confirmed an ongoing SEC investigation regarding its past user metrics. This stands in contrast to news earlier this week that it was set to join the S&P 500. Palmer believes the cyberattack to be more of a one-off event and that the SEC's inquiry is "little more than noise that is highly unlikely to have any material impact on any of the drivers of the bullish thesis on the company's stock." Coinbase up 3% premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1401 GMT - Despite expectations that earnings for Canada's big banks have stalled, several are set to lift payouts to shareholders, BMO Capital Markets' Sohrab Movahedi says. The earnings season for banks kicks off May 22 starting with Toronto-Dominion Bank. Movahedi reckons the Big 5 banks, excluding Bank of Montreal, will turn in flat cash operating EPS following three consecutive quarters of double-digit year-on-year growth. The analyst expects credit quality and reserve adequacy to be a key focus this quarter amid macroeconomic uncertainty. Still, dividend increases are expected from Bank of Nova Scotia, Royal Bank and National Bank of Canada this quarter, Movahedi says. BMO has outperform calls on Royal Bank, TD Bank and Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce for their defensive qualities. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

1233 GMT - Coinbase Global is facing a challenge or two but nothing that should substantially impact its position as one of the primary beneficiaries of institutional adoption of crypto, Benchmark's Mark Palmer says in a research note. The U.S. cryptocurrency exchange Thursday disclosed a significant cybersecurity breach and confirmed an ongoing SEC investigation regarding its past user metrics. This stands in contrast to news earlier this week that it was set to join the S&P 500. Palmer believes the cyberattack to be more of a one-off event and that the SEC's inquiry is "little more than noise that is highly unlikely to have any material impact on any of the drivers of the bullish thesis on the company's stock." Coinbase up 3% premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0914 GMT - Barclays's current valuation in terms of price to earnings and price to tangible net asset value doesn't reflect its true worth and makes the bank the cheapest in Europe, J.P. Morgan Cazenove says in a research note. This seems to be largely due to the negative investment sentiment toward the U.S., where Barclays operates its investment banking and consumer businesses which bring in a third of group revenue, analysts Kian Abouhossein and Sheel Shah write. This view is incorrect as investment banking is the best growth segment thanks to expected high levels of volatility, they say. The U.S. credit card business could face some difficulties but it is a relatively small contributor to earnings, they add. Shares trade 1% higher at 325 pence and have risen 21% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0850 GMT - Investors drew the wrong conclusions from Hannover Re's first-quarter print, Jefferies says in a research note following share weakness earlier this week. This wasn't a low-quality beat with currency gains offsetting softer underwriting, Philip Kett writes. Instead, the German reinsurer "opts to delay the emergence of one-off gains in earnings, trading it for balance sheet prudence that can smooth volatility later," he adds. This is a strategy management has used before, Kett adds. The long-term consequences of this delayed earnings recognition pattern is the accumulation of resilience reserves above the required levels, he adds. Shares recoup recent losses and trade up 1.7% at 280.8 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0827 GMT - The removal of the ring-fencing regime for U.K. banks could save around 1.5 billion pounds for the sector by scrapping operational, funding and other costs, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. The CEOs of some of the country's top lenders wrote to the government asking it to abolish this regime, which requires banks with a certain level of core deposits to legally separate their retail banking activities from other operations. "We estimate that circa 160 billion pounds of excess liquidity is currently sitting in U.K. ring-fenced banks," analysts write, noting that this high quantity is likely due to several factors such as years of quantitative easing and higher savings since the pandemic. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0821 GMT - Standard Chartered's corporate and investment bank is in a good position within its geographical network, J.P. Morgan Cazenove says. The CIB has a strong leadership team that has refocused the unit on returns, operating leverage and risk-weighed asset optimization, JPM Cazenove says in a research note after attending a webinar with its management. "We came away constructive on growth opportunities including deeper wallet penetration with material income multipliers as clients utilize more products/markets, and targeted focus on network and financial institutional income," analysts write. The targets outlined for the unit broadly align with expectations. StanChart is well-placed as supply chains adjust, though its shares are exposed to volatility until tariff uncertainty clears, they add. Shares in London have risen 16% since the start of the year. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1937 GMT - Oil futures end the week on a positive note, lifted mostly by easing trade tensions after the U.S. and China agreed to cut tariffs on each other's goods for 90 days to allow for talks. Keeping a lid on prices despite stronger summer demand approaching are OPEC+ production plans and U.S. talks on Iran's nuclear program. President Trump's optimism that a deal can be reached with Tehran raises the possibility of the U.S. lifting sanctions on Iranian oil. "The market reacted to the president's words seemingly with the view that it could lead to more Iranian oil flowing onto the market," says Anthony Rapa, co-chair of Blank Rome's international trade practice. That would "have a positive impact on prices from the Trump perspective, which is that they want prices to be lower," he says. WTI settles up 1.4% at $62.49 a barrel for a 2.4% weekly gain. Brent rises 1.4% to $65.41 a barrel, up 2.3% on the week. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1723 GMT - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. slipped by one this week to 473 in a third consecutive weekly decline, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. Rigs are at their lowest level since January and down by 24 from a year ago. Production has held up as companies focus on the most productive wells, but "we're really not seeing a lot of investment in new fields," says Vincent Deluard, director of global macro strategy at StoneX. "The whole drill baby drill, it's not about permits...it's really about price. At this point it doesn't make sense to drill at these prices." At the same time, energy companies are being a lot more disciplined about allocating capital, he adds. Rigs directed at natural gas were down by 1 this week at 100, three fewer than a year ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1238 GMT - Oil futures are little changed, looking to post a second straight weekly gain on easing trade tensions that lifted prices early in the week. Back-and-forth on U.S.-Iran nuclear talks buffeted prices, as President Trump followed threats of "massive maximum pressure," against Tehran with expressions of optimism about reaching a deal, while Treasury sanctioned more firms involved in Iranian oil trade. Global crude stocks increased, including a 3.5 million barrel build in the U.S., OPEC held its demand growth estimate at 1.3 million barrels a day, while the IEA sees a looser balance in raising its supply forecast by more than demand. WTI and Brent are up 0.1% at $61.67 and $64.60 a barrel, respectively.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1029 GMT - Orsted's valuation reflects a worst-case scenario, in which it would be ordered to halt work on its U.S. offshore projects, which is unlikely, UBS analyst Mark Freshney writes. Shares have fallen 37% year-to-date, reflecting the risk of a halt order on its Revolution and Sunrise projects, as was the case at Equinor's Empire Wind. "We view a halt order on the U.S. offshore projects as less-likely, given there is a local equity partner in Revolution Wind, high state of completion and local content." Revolution offshore works are to be completed early 2026 and Sunrise early 2027, which also removes risk, he adds. UBS lowers its price target to 400 Danish kroner from 420 kroner, and keeps at buy. Shares rise 0.7% to 246.40 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1837 GMT - Gold's front-month contract settles down 1.2% to $3,182 a troy ounce. For the week, the contract declined 4.6%, the largest weekly percentage drop since June 2021. Growing interest in re-engaging in equities and other assets have weighed on gold, but whether there's more short-term downside remains unclear. "Amid the trade onslaught, the probability of a recession has risen substantially," says the World Gold Council in a note. The organization says investors should keep in mind that considerable downside risk remains for other asset classes, "leading to greater safe-haven demand, notably gold." (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1714 GMT - Emirates Global Aluminum plans to build a primary aluminum smelter outside of Tulsa, the company announced with a memorandum of understanding signed with the state of Oklahoma. If completed, it would be the first aluminum smelter opened in the U.S. in 45 years, Emirates says. The announcement is part of $200 billion of investments by the United Arab Emirates announced by the White House yesterday. This investment is valued at $4 billion. EGA is a joint venture between two government entities in the Middle East, and is among the biggest producers of primary aluminum globally. Tariffs imposed by the Trump Administration are in part meant to reinvigorate domestic aluminum in the U.S., with companies like Century Aluminum Co. and Alcoa struggling to keep their older primary smelters open. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0938 GMT - Cyclical stocks should outperform in the current environment and European basic resources, real estate and autos have room for a further rally, Citi equity strategists say in a research note. The strategists cite Glencore, BMW, Pandora, Schneider Electric and Vonovia, among others, as beaten-up stocks for which analysts are dialing up earnings forecasts. Markets have moved swiftly higher as trade tensions between the U.S. and China eased and the likelihood of a recession receded, suggesting earnings uncertainty is already past its peak, Citi says. Nevertheless, there are still some cyclical stocks left, the strategists say. The Stoxx Europe 600 index rises 0.6%, having gained 2.3% this week. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1805 GMT - Scotiabank says Automotive Properties Real Estate Investment Trust underperformed other REITS by 5 points from early February to early April in the midst of tariff headlines, but "now, APR is back to pre-tariff levels, and back to being a steady-eddy high distribution yield REIT with high cash flow visibility." Analyst Himanshu Gupta says some near term uncertainties on dealer M&A activity continue due to lingering tariff concerns, but Gupta notes that management said the auto dealer community was buoyant during March and April. "They see the demand for future acquisitions, activity is expected to improve mid to late in the year," Gupta adds.(adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1435 GMT - Several indicators support the view that the U.S. airline industry is in the midst of an earnings recession, Deutsche analysts say in a research note. The analysts estimate system passenger volumes and average fares are trending lower, resulting in lower top lines. Demand appears to be down the most in domestic markets among price-sensitive customers, they say. International travel is still strong, though some conflicts abroad are denting certain markets: demand to travel to India has been in a free fall amid regional hostilities and Delta and United extended the suspension of their services to Israel, the analysts note. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1149 GMT - German carmakers' first-quarter results were a drag on overall earnings growth for companies in the DAX blue-chip index, equity and cross-asset strategists at Deutsche Bank say in a research note. On aggregate, first-quarter earnings at DAX constituents fell 8% compared with the year-earlier period and exceeded estimates by 5%, Deutsche Bank says. However, the picture is more positive when excluding the auto sector--earnings ere up by 4% and beat forecasts by 8%, according to Deutsche Bank. For 2025 as a whole, the auto sector is expected to weigh on the DAX as well, as the consensus puts earnings growth at 4% when including autos and 11% excluding the sector, the strategists say. The DAX is up 0.5%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1045 GMT - Renk Group's top-line outlook is very strong, JPMorgan analysts David Perry and Lucy Fitzgerald say. JPM upgrades its rating on the stock to overweight from neutral because the German defense group's performance in the two most recent quarters has improved after the resignation of the CFO and CEO, they say. "Given the extremely strong outlook for European defense spending, especially in Germany, and very strong recent orders for armored vehicles containing Renk's products, the future looks very bright for Renk, in our view," the analysts write in a note to investors. Shares are up 8.5% at 60.21 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1012 GMT - Stellantis CFO Doug Osterman has again reiterated the potential for a company recovery, although current North American dynamics and tariff uncertainty allow for little detail at this time, Citi analysts write. The comments come after Osterman spoke at a fireside chat, Citi says. It seems likely that first-half earnings will remain at low levels, but after that, Stellantis sees significant recovery potential, the bank adds. Tariff risks remain substantial, but the company also has opportunities to maximize U.S. production and profitability of new U.S. products. The 3.75% tariff exemption on parts also adds valuable time to localize supply chains. "Stellantis remains at deep value earnings and valuation levels." Citi retains its 9 euro target price and neutral rating. Shares rise 0.2% to 9.59 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0938 GMT - Cyclical stocks should outperform in the current environment and European basic resources, real estate and autos have room for a further rally, Citi equity strategists say in a research note. The strategists cite Glencore, BMW, Pandora, Schneider Electric and Vonovia, among others, as beaten-up stocks for which analysts are dialing up earnings forecasts. Markets have moved swiftly higher as trade tensions between the U.S. and China eased and the likelihood of a recession receded, suggesting earnings uncertainty is already past its peak, Citi says. Nevertheless, there are still some cyclical stocks left, the strategists say. The Stoxx Europe 600 index rises 0.6%, having gained 2.3% this week. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0913 GMT - Singapore Airlines' earnings momentum faces multiple headwinds, the DBS Group Research team writes in a note. While forward bookings are positive so far, pricing pressures are likely to persist, given the global uncertainty, they write. Cost inflation is expected to remain elevated. Scoot, its low-cost carrier, particularly has faced intense competition, they add. Cargo is likely to perform better-than-expected in the near-term, thanks to front-loading ahead of potential tariffs, they say. SIA faced significant margin contraction in 4Q given cost pressures from higher-than-expected ex-fuel expenditure, but lower fuel prices will likely provide some relief in FY26, they write. DBS has a hold rating on the stock with a target price of S$6.30. Shares are 0.15% higher at S$6.89. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

data.title: Global polymer market synopsis
data.description: AsiaHDPE: This week, HDPE prices down adjusted in parts of South Asia while quoting stable in other parts of the Asian region. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. In China, favourable macroeconomic conditions have persisted, resulting in an increasingly conducive market environment. Downstream manufacturers, motivated by the need to replenish stocks, have increased their acquisitions, especially at lower price levels. This has contributed to enhanced supply-demand conditions, with the polyethylene market remaining robust despite varying trends. In the U.S. dollar-denominated market, there has been a moderate rise in prices, with market quotes reflecting a rise from the previous period. The reduction in tariff tensions between China and the U.S. has stimulated terminal foreign trade orders, providing support for U.S. dollar prices. Nevertheless, the diminishing price differential between domestic and international markets has effectively closed the import window for most product categories. Forward-priced U.S. shipments experienced a slight increase, and overall market sentiment has improved compared to earlier periods. However, the actual effects will hinge on customs declaration results following May 13. Looking forward, the broader market outlook remains weak in spite of short-term optimism, with the U.S. dollar market likely to continue exhibiting signs of weakness. In Southeast Asia, the polyethylene market has shown stability, bolstered by positive macroeconomic developments that have enhanced market sentiment. The introduction of favourable tariff policies has significantly increased confidence among traders and investors. This optimistic outlook has prompted traders to strategically reduce their inventory levels, aiming to take advantage of the favourable conditions or to manage their stock effectively. The market has experienced a positive trend due to these encouraging macroeconomic factors, while lower import offers have been retracted. Last week, several major suppliers from the Middle East have nearly depleted their current allocations and are now anticipating the release of new quotas for June shipments. These new quotas are expected to be announced in the last week of May, potentially impacting market dynamics upon their release. The current stability of the market is primarily attributed to supply limitations resulting from the temporary withdrawal of lower offers and the forthcoming availability of new import options. Nevertheless, trading activity is projected to increase in the latter part of May as new offers for June-loading cargoes start to surface. In India, the domestic polyethylene (PE) market continues to exhibit a generally weak sentiment, primarily due to a lack of robust buying interest. PE grades, except LDPE grades, have remained relatively stable owing to limited market availability. Most purchasers have chosen to restrict their acquisitions to immediate needs, primarily sourcing from local manufacturers. This cautious strategy is a reflection of the prevailing pessimistic market outlook. Domestic suppliers have made efforts to boost demand by providing price incentives; however, anticipated changes in trade flows are likely to limit short-term import demand. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, the majority of buyers opted to remain inactive, preferring a passive role instead of engaging in the market. This prudent position is largely attributed to their wait-and-see strategy, as they are anticipating new import offers from overseas suppliers. LDPE: This week, Low density polyethylene prices journeyed southward in the Asian region. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. In China, the LDPE market is currently characterized by weak demand, as purchasers are either engaging in minimal buying or restricting themselves to inquiries. The overall market sentiment is subdued, influenced in part by the slow recovery of demand and ongoing geopolitical and trade tensions between the United States and China, leading many buyers to take a cautious, wait-and-see stance. In Vietnam, the LDPE market continues to exhibit weak trends. Nevertheless, import offers to Vietnam and Malaysia have remained stable for the second consecutive week, leading to speculation that the recent downward trend may be approaching a cyclical low. However, buyers are still placing bids at lower levels, and a few transactions completed at these prices have further intensified the downward pressure on the market. In India, market sentiment has remained distinctly pessimistic as buyers have largely halted their purchases in response to a significant price decline. To boost demand, domestic producers have introduced various pre- and post-sales discount schemes. Despite this, buyers have remained cautious, delaying purchases in expectation of additional price corrections. Local suppliers have also implemented price protection strategies to bolster order flows, yet overall demand has remained tepid. Processors are operating with limited stockpiles and adopting a wait-and-see approach, influenced by ongoing price volatility. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, the majority of purchasers have preferred to remain on the sidelines, exercising caution while they await new import offers from international suppliers. This cautious stance is indicative of a wider wait-and-see approach in light of the ongoing market volatility. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices slipped in Asia. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. In China, prices of LLDPE have decreased, mainly due to reduced offers from international suppliers, even though favourable macroeconomic conditions have persisted, contributing to a more positive market environment. Downstream manufacturers, driven by the need to replenish stocks, have increased their purchases, especially at lower price levels. This has strengthened the supply-demand balance, keeping the polyethylene market robust despite varying trends. In the U.S. dollar market, prices have seen a partial rise, with market quotes increasing relative to the previous period. The easing of tariff tensions between China and the U.S. has enhanced foreign trade orders, thereby supporting U.S. dollar prices. Nevertheless, the diminishing price disparity between domestic and global markets has effectively closed the import opportunities for most product categories. Today, forward-priced U.S. shipments have seen a minor increase, and overall market sentiment has improved compared to earlier periods. However, the true effect will hinge on the outcomes of customs declarations after May 13. Despite short-term optimism, the broader market outlook remains weak, and the U.S. dollar market may continue to show signs of weakness. In Southeast Asia's Linear Low Density Polyethylene (LLDPE) import market, prices have decreased while market activity has remained low, characterized by limited offers and transactions. Overall demand is weak, as numerous plastic converters expect a decline in orders for finished products, especially from U.S. clients due to ongoing trade tensions and tariffs. Most major suppliers from the Middle East have exhausted their current allocations and are now anticipating new quotas for June shipments, which are expected to be announced in the last week of May. In Vietnam, purchasing sentiment is sluggish, influenced by the ongoing availability of competitively priced U.S.-origin cargoes, some of which have been redirected from China. In Indonesia, a significant price reduction by a key regional producer has further weakened sentiment, leading buyers to lower their bids and postpone purchases. Nevertheless, trading activity may increase in the latter half of May as new offers for June-loading cargoes start to surface. In India, the LLDPE market has generally exhibited weakness, primarily due to a lack of buying interest. Prices for LLDPE have fallen to a three-month low, while other polyethylene grades have maintained relative stability owing to limited market supply. Most purchasers have restricted their acquisitions to immediate needs, predominantly sourcing from local manufacturers. This cautious approach stems from a generally pessimistic market outlook. Domestic suppliers have sought to boost demand through price incentives; however, anticipated changes in trade flows are likely to limit short-term import demand. Additionally, escalating geopolitical tensions contribute to market uncertainty, potentially affecting regional shipping operations and increasing freight costs. Domestic buying activity has remained subdued, with buyers awaiting the announcement of new Memorandums of Understanding (MOUs) and schemes from Indian producers for the new financial year. Market participants anticipate an increase in spot buying by mid to late April, following the release of MOUs and the signing of new contracts. Sentiment in the market has been cautious as stakeholders await government funding announcements and tender releases for infrastructure projects, which are deemed essential for enhancing demand for compound grades in April and May, prior to the monsoon season that typically hampers construction activities nationwide. Indian producers have already introduced discount schemes for May 2025. Nevertheless, weak demand from end-users and sufficient product availability in the domestic market have led to a cautious market perspective. Consequently, most traders have adopted a wait-and-see strategy amid ongoing uncertainty and price volatility. Even though demand remains tepid, the Indian polyethylene market remains adequately supplied, with no adverse impact foreseen on material avails in spite of two scheduled plant turnarounds this month. Given the ample availability, buyers are unlikely to encounter any supply-side pressures. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, the market undertone was largely muted with hardly any trading activity witnessed as participants awaited fresh offers. PP: This week, Polypropylene prices down adjusted in the Asian region. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. Chinese cargoes have been withdrawn from the market, and buyers are now awaiting fresh July shipment offers. Recent macroeconomic trends have created a more optimistic market atmosphere. The introduction of favourable tariff policies has notably boosted confidence among traders and investors, leading to a more optimistic outlook. Consequently, buyers are currently refraining from making purchases as they monitor the evolving market conditions and await new offers from international suppliers. Prices marched higher in Far east Asia and Southeast Asia following the recent reduction in tensions between China and the United States which has enhanced overall sentiment within the commodity markets. The rise in crude oil prices has further bolstered confidence throughout the petrochemical sector. On the supply front, the polypropylene (PP) market is still facing tight conditions due to ongoing maintenance at production facilities. The decrease in production levels has alleviated inventory pressures, providing fundamental support for prices. However, demand is currently weak as the market enters a seasonal slowdown. Limited orders from downstream sectors and a lack of enthusiasm for procuring raw materials have restricted the potential for substantial price hikes. Nevertheless, encouraging macroeconomic news�especially the introduction of favorable tariff regulations�has uplifted market sentiment. Traders are actively selling off their inventories, although downstream purchasers are primarily concentrating on meeting only their immediate needs. Raw material consumption remains inadequate, prompting upstream manufacturers to persist in reducing their stock levels. Interest in replenishment from downstream buyers is limited, and the growth of orders is sluggish. Nonetheless, ongoing supply constraints due to significant maintenance-related losses continue to provide price support, indicating that the polypropylene market is likely to remain stable to firm in the near future. In India, the prices of polypropylene (PP) have stabilized following a decline over the previous five weeks, attributed to limited demand for imported materials. Consequently, market discussions have been minimal. Processors have primarily focused on purchasing based on necessity, preferring domestic sources and maintaining a cautious approach in light of the prevailing bearish market conditions. The price incentives provided by local suppliers have enhanced the appeal of domestic materials. Looking forward, a decline in downstream activities across significant sectors is anticipated to negatively affect PP demand in the upcoming weeks. Sectors such as raffia bags utilized in construction and agriculture, as well as rigid packaging applications including paints, ice creams, and beverages, are expected to see a decrease in PP demand. Additionally, the rising geopolitical tensions in South Asian region are being closely monitored by participants, as they may disrupt shipping operations and lead to higher freight costs. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, the majority of purchasers opted to remain on the sidelines, taking a prudent stance as they anticipate new import offers from overseas suppliers. This cautious approacj signifies a calculated strategy in light of prevailing market ambiguities. PS: Polystyrene prices continued to be steady in Asia this week. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. China's import demand has remained consistent, as local shipments are still offered at lower prices compared to imports from other Asian countries. Conversely, there has been a notable increase in competitive PS offers from China in various regions, including Southeast and South Asia, as Chinese exporters aim to clear excess inventory and broaden their sales activities in international markets. The fundamentals of the Southeast Asian spot PS market have remained mostly stable compared to the prior week, with an ongoing oversupply situation. Certain market participants observed a slight uptick in spot demand relative to the previous week, potentially influenced by early-month procurement activities. However, the demand for general purpose polystyrene (GPPS) in Southeast Asia has remained relatively consistent, bolstered by its various downstream applications. ABS: ABS prices quoted lower in South Asia, while remaining stable in Far east Asia and Southeast Asia this week. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. The Asian ABS market exhibited stability in both China and Southeast Asia, with prices in China reflecting tradable indications while Southeast Asia maintained a slight premium over China. Market sentiment remained cautious due to elevated inventories in China and the commencement of new production units, which contributed to the existing capacity. A producer indicated that although upstream styrene monomer prices are on the rise, demand is anticipated to remain weak in the short term. This is primarily because numerous downstream buyers depend on exports, and the ongoing uncertainties surrounding international tariff negotiations are causing them to postpone their purchases. Upstream markets displayed a general upward trend, with notable gains observed in key feedstocks. In the South Asian region, ABS prices have decreased despite the rising values of upstream styrene, butadiene, and ACN. The current market dynamics indicate a price decline primarily driven by reduced import offers, which have negatively impacted overall price levels. However, these lower offers are now being retracted as suppliers move away from earlier low-price strategies. Recent macroeconomic trends have created a more optimistic market atmosphere. Importantly, the introduction of beneficial tariff policies has greatly improved confidence among traders and investors, leading to a more favorable outlook. PVC: This week, PVC prices quoted flat across the Asian region. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. The spot market for polyvinyl chloride (PVC) in Asia witnessed a decline in trading activity. This decelerated momentum was mainly attributed to the fact that most buyers had already restocked their inventories for May, resulting in a diminished urgency to acquire further supplies. Consequently, trading volumes remained low. During this time, market participants redirected their attention to potential factors that could affect PVC negotiations and pricing for the upcoming June shipments. Overall, the market's cautious stance indicates a temporary halt in immediate trading, with focus shifting to elements that may influence future market dynamics. Additionally, some market participants observed that the anticipated monsoon seasonal slowdown in India and certain Southeast Asian nations in Q3 could further affect demand. In China, PVC prices exhibited a notable degree of stability. Domestic prices remained confined within a limited range, bolstered by continuous restocking efforts following the holiday season. This replenishment activity post-holiday contributed to the maintenance of price stability, even in the face of market activity fluctuations. Concurrently, export prices for PVC from the region also remained consistent, despite a downturn in export demand. The consistent export pricing indicates that suppliers chose to uphold their price levels in light of diminished overseas demand, potentially to preserve market share or in anticipation of a future recovery in demand. In summary, the market experienced a phase of price stability across both domestic and export sectors amidst varying supply-demand conditions. In India, trade sources have reported that discussions regarding exports have been muted, as buyers are taking a cautious wait-and-see approach. This reluctance stems from the current market volatility and the growing uncertainty related to forthcoming regulatory changes in India. In particular, there are rising concerns about the anticipated enforcement of PVC import restrictions in India, which may include antidumping duties and regulations imposed by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS). These potential trade obstacles are prompting buyers to refrain from making commitments, resulting in limited negotiations and a careful market atmosphere as stakeholders brace for the upcoming regulatory changes. The seller noted that various factors are currently impeding PVC exports from India. They specifically highlighted that the imminent monsoon season poses logistical and operational challenges, likely due to weather-related disruptions that could impact transportation and manufacturing processes. Moreover, they pointed out that antidumping duties designed to shield domestic industries from unfairly priced imports are creating financial barriers, rendering Indian PVC less competitive in global markets. Additionally, the controls enforced by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS), which entail strict quality and safety regulations, introduce regulatory challenges that exporters must address, potentially leading to increased compliance costs and delays. Together, these elements�seasonal weather challenges, trade protection measures, and regulatory requirements�are currently hindering the growth and facilitation of PVC exports from India. PET: This week, PET prices experienced sharp gains in the Asian region. The rise in PET prices within the region was influenced by a variety of interrelated factors. Firstly, increased upstream energy prices led to elevated production costs throughout the supply chain. Furthermore, the prices of essential feedstocks such as MEG, PTA, and PX experienced significant increases. Given that these feedstocks are critical raw materials for PET production, their heightened costs directly affected the overall manufacturing expenses. As the prices of MEG, PTA, and PX rose, producers encountered increased input costs, which they subsequently passed on to the market. In order to maintain their profit margins and avert financial losses, producers are necessitated to pass these elevated costs onto end consumers by increasing the selling prices of PET. This price modification is crucial for safeguarding profit margins in light of the rising raw material costs. The downstream polyester yarn and fiber market has also continued to rise, propelled by the increasing costs of upstream feedstocks. Nevertheless, there has been minimal discourse or activity in the regional market concerning these changes. Meanwhile, the temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. The previous day's offer for PET bottle-grade, from a Chinese producer, was pegged at the USD 790�795/mt on FOB basis. Following discussions early morning, the producer officially increased its offer to around USD 820-825/mt on FOB basis. This marks an increase of approximately USD 30/mt from the previously stated range. Despite this upward adjustment, there have been no signs or reports of negotiations or discussions taking place at the new offer price level. Polyethylene Terephthalate bottle grade is experiencing a favourable trend within the polyester industry, propelled by favourable macroeconomic policies and seasonal demand. The increase in upstream raw material costs, heightened operating rates, and a rise in both domestic and international orders suggest that China's PET sector is exhibiting resilience and stability in its supply chain. The People's Bank of China implemented interest rate and reserve requirement reductions in early May that surpassed market expectations, thereby providing robust support to the real economy and capital markets. Concurrently, a positive advancement in China-U.S. trade relations has further revitalized export-oriented industries. In South East Asia, the Ministry of Investment, Trade and Industry (MITI) of Malaysia formally declared the implementation of definitive anti-dumping duties on polyethylene terephthalate (PET) resin imports from China and Indonesia on May 7, 2025. The announcement stated that the anti-dumping investigation, which commenced on August 9, 2024, found that certain PET products were being imported into the Malaysian market at prices lower than those in their respective domestic markets, causing significant harm to Malaysia's local PET industry. Based on the conclusive findings, duty rates varying from 2.29% to 11.74% have been implemented on imports from China while a fixed duty rate of 37.44% has been implemented on imports from Malaysia. These duties will be enforced by the Royal Malaysian Customs Department for a duration of five years, from May 7, 2025, to May 6, 2030. Europe HDPE: This week, HDPE prices trended lower in the European region. The current downward pricing pressure on the European high-density polyethylene (HDPE) market can be attributed to competitive material offers from the United States . According to recent market reports, prices have rapidly dropped over the last week. With a large influx of import offers from both the US and Turkey, injection grades of HDPE have been the most negatively impacted of the different grades. While there have been isolated cases of supply shortages due to delays in some anticipated shipments, these disruptions have had little effect on the dynamics of the market as a whole. Due to a rise in market offers, the price premium between HDPE blow molding and injection grades has also decreased. Participants in the market have frequently described demand as being weak, especially in the spot market where there is a clear lack of desire to purchase more volumes. Demand has been steady in the packaging industry, but it is still low in other derivative segments, particularly in the construction and automotive industries. Contractual negotiations have been ongoing for the past week. According to reports, some converters have asked for price reductions that are more significant than those brought about by the drop in monomer prices. The general mood of the market suggests that more price reductions are desired, especially from companies that did not offer reductions in April and are now facing an influx of material. Consequently, these producers are becoming more and more willing to negotiate. Reductions equal to the complete monomer decrease have been provided by certain domestic producers. However, with most market participants opting to postpone settlements until the end of the month in the hopes of better terms, few finalized contract prices have been reported. The European polyethylene (PE) market is expected to face continuous challenges, with demand expected to remain muted, in addition to immediate restocking requirements. This prediction is based on a stagnant economy where the risks of recession run high. With the exception of a few applications related to fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) and the comparatively robust flexible packaging industry, most buyers are expected to limit their purchases to necessary quantities and avoid making speculative purchases. Although it is too soon to tell, a backwardated pricing structure for polyethylene might result from a large increase in upstream crude oil production and the ensuing effect on feedstock prices. In the event that this happens, buyers might decide to further reduce their current stockpiles with the goal of restocking at a more advantageous price in the upcoming months. There is still uncertainty about whether the market has levelled off at a lower threshold, indicating the formation of a new equilibrium, or whether the modest recovery is just the result of front-loading during the 90-day EU-US reciprocal tariff suspension. In the UK, where companies are stepping up cost-cutting efforts in the face of worsening manufacturing conditions, the outlook is still especially dire. The situation is being made worse by weak domestic consumer confidence and dwindling export demand, which is forcing businesses to cut back on production. Spot prices for all grades of polyethylene have generally decreased throughout the week. Although some sellers have not yet provided final offers, several have already enacted substantial price reductions on medium- to large-volume orders set for delivery in May. These decreases, which reflect considerable discounts relative to previous spot prices in May, highlight the varied sentiments among sellers. Nevertheless, additional confirmation of these price trends is required, as the market looks for greater clarity in the upcoming weeks. LDPE: This week, LDPE prices journeyed southward in the European region. The European low-density polyethylene (LDPE) market has continued to experience a decline in prices over the past week, primarily influenced by competitive pricing from the United States. Reports suggest that prices are falling swiftly, as the influx of imported materials puts additional strain on domestic markets. The LDPE sector is particularly affected, facing the most considerable price reductions due to robust import offers from both the US and Turkey, which heighten competition. Although some expected shipments have faced delays, leading to localized supply constraints, these interruptions have had a negligible effect on the overall market dynamics. Additionally, the price differential between various polyethylene grades has diminished as a result of an increase in market offers, further exacerbating competitive pressures. Demand continues to be characterized as weak, especially in the spot market, where the desire for additional volumes is notably low. In the derivatives sector, packaging demand has remained relatively stable; however, consumption in the automotive and construction industries is still sluggish, indicative of broader economic uncertainties. Ongoing contractual negotiations have seen some converters seeking greater reductions than those corresponding with the decline in monomer prices. Overall, market sentiment indicates that those who previously avoided price reductions in April are now more willing to engage in negotiations due to increasing material availability. While certain domestic producers have implemented full reductions in accordance with the monomer price drop, few finalized contract prices have been reported. Many participants in the market are delaying deal closures, opting to wait until later in the month in hopes of achieving more advantageous pricing as the supply situation continues to change. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices slipped sharply in the European region. This week, the European linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE) market faced renewed downward pressure due to weak demand and a rise in imports from the United States. The situation was exacerbated by a ¬70/tonne reduction in the feedstock ethylene monthly contract price (MCP) for May, which further impacted LLDPE spot prices. Nevertheless, a clearer perspective on contractual pricing has yet to emerge, as numerous negotiations are still in progress or have not yet started. There was notable aggressive selling activity across all LLDPE grades, particularly from distributors looking to reduce excess inventories. This influx of discounted offers is occurring despite May generally being a seasonal restocking period for various downstream sectors in anticipation of the upcoming summer slowdown. Sellers have lowered their prices for immediate volumes as a result of increased pressure brought on by the anticipated arrival of competitively priced polyethylene imports from the US in June. In order to prevent possible inventory devaluation, some market participants are rushing to sell off their current stocks because it is anticipated that the replacement costs for these incoming shipments will be much lower. The need to clear excess volumes became more pressing as the week went on, especially for sellers whose stock levels were higher than anticipated. Because sellers now appear more inclined to make compromises in order to close deals, buyers now have more negotiating power in ongoing discussions. There are, nevertheless, some divergent viewpoints in the market. Higher stock level sellers are taking a more aggressive pricing strategy, while some sellers with comparatively balanced inventories are less pressured to provide significant discounts. As the market tries to find equilibrium in the face of shifting supply-demand dynamics, this discrepancy is creating a mixed pricing environment with the potential for additional volatility. PP: This week, PP prices plunged in the European region. The European polypropylene market experienced additional price declines over the week, influenced by sluggish demand and reduced feedstock costs. Concurrently, spot prices plummeted to their lowest point since January 2024, as a surge in import offers led to lower offers from sellers. This situation, coupled with a Euro 65/mt reduction in the May monthly contract price (MCP) for feedstock propylene, has applied downward pressure on PP spot prices. The significant declines may indicate some concern among sellers amid persistently low demand, potentially motivating them to mitigate the risks associated with excess supply on their balance sheets. Demand for spot material remained weak, primarily due to ongoing low demand from key automotive and construction sectors. Although packaging demand held steady, it was not enough to counterbalance the rising supply in the market. Market participants indicated that an increase in demand is unlikely under the current economic conditions. Additionally, uncertainty regarding the implications of US-China tariffs and trade negotiations has led buyers to postpone material purchases. On the supply front, the availability of PP remained robust, exacerbated by subdued activity in the spot market. Competitive offers for materials from the Middle East and Asia further enhanced PP availability. However, logistical challenges are causing delays for both local suppliers and importers. While such conditions might have supported pricing in different circumstances, the current fundamentals, particularly weak demand, are preventing any stabilization of prices. The decline in spot prices occurred despite several plant shutdowns. Normal supply has yet to be restored from polypropylene plants in Spain and Portugal that ceased operations following a sudden power outage on April 28. Although some of these facilities are slowly coming back online, their operational status remains uncertain. Additionally, a cracker in northern France is still offline and has declared force majeure regarding the supply of feedstock propylene. However, the nearby polypropylene plant has resumed production by sourcing propylene through imports. Furthermore, a polypropylene plant in the Netherlands is set to begin a scheduled turnaround in the last week of May, which is expected to last until the end of June. Market participants noted minimal differences in the performance of copolymer and homopolymer injection-grade materials, although the price gap between the two grades widened due to residual localized demand for copolymer. Contract pricing has also experienced downward pressure due to the reduction in the May propylene monthly contract price. The market aims to transfer the complete decline in the monomer price to polypropylene producers, who are attempting to maintain their pricing position; however, the healthy supply complicates their efforts. The realization of such pricing targets remains uncertain, particularly if weak demand persists in the upcoming weeks. Conversely, buyers are focused on ensuring that the full decrease in monomer prices is reflected in their polypropylene contracts. Some buyers anticipate that polypropylene contract prices may face additional pressure later in May, especially if there is an oversupply in the market. Despite the decline in spot prices increasing the disparity with contract prices, producers are of the opinion that it is essential for polypropylene (PP) contract margins to remain stable. They highlight the challenges posed by low margins and a demand landscape that jeopardizes the financial viability of their operations in Europe, which are already at a competitive disadvantage compared to other regions. This situation is underscored by a series of asset evaluations and recent announcements of permanent closures. The most recent development occurred, with a producer reporting a halt in the refurbishment of its Wilton cracker in the UK, attributing the decision to economic uncertainty and difficult market conditions Concurrently, trade tensions between the US and EU present an additional layer of uncertainty for market stakeholders. On May 8, the European Commission initiated a public consultation regarding a list of US imports, alongside those already designated for tariff implementation, which may face EU countermeasures if the ongoing negotiations do not yield a favourable resolution and the US tariffs are not lifted. Polypropylene imports from the US were included in this consultation list. The EU does not rely heavily on US-sourced PP imports, as the price differentials often render this trade unviable. The consultation period will remain open until June 10, after which a final proposal for countermeasures will be drafted, along with a legal framework for their enforcement should negotiations with the US fail to achieve satisfactory outcomes. This development can be interpreted in various ways; however, it may be contended that the announcement itself does not indicate any significant progress between the two parties thus far. Nevertheless, should negotiations lead to a breakthrough, particularly concerning automotive exports, it could enhance PP demand in Europe from that sector, which has been adversely affected by low demand and challenging market conditions. PS: This week, PS contract prices drifted lower, while spot prices remaining flat in the European region. The contract values for polystyrene have experienced a week-over-week decline, mirroring the decrease in the European styrene May contract price, which has settled at Euro 40/mt. Despite the reduction in polystyrene prices, imports are reported to remain competitively priced, with a distributor sourcing North African material for the production of extruded polystyrene, commonly used for insulation in the construction sector. Demand for May is expected to remain stable overall, similar to April, with no signs of significant fluctuations. However, minor variations are noted among downstream sectors, particularly influenced by seasonal factors from the construction industry in spring. Meanwhile, demand in packaging remains steady, while automotive demand is weak. Market participants anticipate that polystyrene demand is likely to remain flat and stable for the remainder of 2025. Downstream sectors continue to be influenced by macroeconomic factors, including inflation and interest rates, with recent inflation rates remaining steady and elevated despite some reductions observed thus far in 2025. Supply remains balanced to long, as producers adjust outputs in line with demand. Uncertainty lingers regarding the potential impacts of US tariffs on the supply chain, with market participants awaiting further information. Additionally, upstream styrene production faces challenges, as a styrene facility in Gonfreville is undergoing maintenance, and there is also maintenance at the joint venture Ellba. Furthermore, a force majeure has been declared at the propylene oxide/styrene monomer (POSM) plant in Tarragona, along with the permanent closure of a plant in Maasvlakte, the Netherlands, scheduled for October 2025. The effects of these upstream production issues on downstream polystyrene remain uncertain, and industry players are closely tracking the situation. Alongside competitive pricing, the steady reduction in container freight rates has opened up arbitrage opportunities between Asia and Europe. Nevertheless, in spite of these advantageous pricing conditions and lower freight rates, imports continue to face delays, requiring buyers to place orders for necessary quantities two months in advance. These delays are linked to the rerouting of vessels around the Cape of Good Hope, a result of Houthi rebel attacks on merchant ships in the Red Sea since December 2023. Previously, vessels would typically transit through the Suez Canal in the Red Sea. Additionally, the delays in deliveries have also impacted the transport of materials from the Mediterranean Sea to Europe. The market for expanded polystyrene (EPS) contracts experienced a decrease in prices on a month-to-month basis; however, the reductions were not as pronounced as those observed in GPPS. This decline in prices was linked to fluctuations in the styrene spot market, which plays a crucial role in determining EPS pricing, as nearly 60% of EPS production in Europe depends on purchases of spot styrene cargoes. ABS: This week, ABS contract prices nosedived, while spot prices were left unchanged in the European region. In Europe, the prices of ABS have shown a declining trend, influenced by a notable drop in upstream butadiene prices and a decrease of Euro 40/mt in the May contract price for European styrene. This decline was further exacerbated by a bearish market climate. At the same time, high production levels in the region led to competitive market offers. The appliance and automotive sectors, which are significant consumers of ABS, saw only a slight recovery, while the UK's industrial production continued to decline. Although some companies have implemented production cuts and facility shutdowns, supply has consistently outstripped demand, resulting in reduced margins and profitability. The high energy costs in Europe, along with structural inefficiencies, have created additional obstacles for production. Despite initial expectations for a modest increase in manufacturing indices, demand from the automotive and appliance industries in Europe has not shown substantial improvement. Supply remains strong, and distributors in the European ABS market maintain a largely pessimistic view of market demand. The oversupply situation has prompted aggressive market offers and heightened competition, while local production levels have remained high despite weak demand fundamentals.
